items.question_text: If traffic conditions permit, what may you approve in Class C or Class D surface areas?

choices.text: A pilot's request to cross the surface area or exceed the surface-area speed limit, subject to the 250-knot cap unless the pilot reports a higher required minimum speed.
Any unusual maneuver the pilot wants to perform as long as the pilot remains in two-way communications.
Any speed the pilot requests, including well above 250 knots, without further conditions.
A buzz job if it shortens the arrival path and reduces pattern congestion.

items.explanation: Crossing the surface area or exceeding the normal speed limit may be approved if traffic permits, but the 250-knot cap and higher-minimum-speed exception still apply.

items.instruction: Is this surface-area speed condition correct?
items.question_text: If traffic conditions permit, you may approve a request to exceed the Class C or Class D surface-area speed limit, but not above 250 knots unless the pilot tells you a higher minimum speed is required.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph allows a speed-limit exception when traffic permits, but not above 250 knots unless the pilot reports a higher minimum speed requirement.

items.question_text: Do not approve a pilot's request or ask a pilot to conduct unusual maneuvers within Class B, C, or D surface areas if they are not essential to the performance of the flight.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Unusual maneuvers in surface areas are barred unless they are essential to the flight.

items.question_text: The unusual-maneuver exception applies only when the activity is conducted in accordance with a letter of agreement and will have no adverse effect on safety or reduce service to other users.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The exception is narrow and requires both LOA coverage and no adverse operational effect.

items.instruction: Is this note-based speed statement correct?
items.question_text: A speed above 250 knots may be approved only if the pilot informs you that a higher minimum speed is required.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph and note together preserve the 250-knot ceiling unless the pilot reports a required higher minimum speed.

items.situation: A military pilot requests to accelerate to 265 knots while crossing the Class D surface area and advises that a higher minimum speed is required by normal military operating procedures. Traffic conditions permit the request. Another controller says the surface-area rule absolutely caps everyone at 250 knots, no exceptions.
items.para_context: If traffic conditions permit, controllers may approve a pilot's request to exceed the Class C or D surface-area speed limit, but not above 250 knots unless the pilot informs the controller that a higher minimum speed is required. The note recognizes AFM and military minimum-speed cases.

choices.text: You may approve the higher speed because traffic permits it and the pilot has stated that a higher minimum speed is required.
You must deny it because the 250-knot limit has no exceptions in surface areas.
You may approve it only if the pilot also agrees to perform unusual maneuvering inside the surface area.
You should delay the request until the pilot is outside the surface area because controllers may never authorize a speed change inside Class D.

items.explanation: The rule allows the higher speed when traffic conditions permit and the pilot reports a required higher minimum speed.

items.question_text: What must local controllers do regarding runway scanning?

choices.text: Visually scan runways to the maximum extent possible.
Rely on automation displays instead of visual scanning whenever a display is available.
Scan only the departure runway, leaving all arrival-runway scanning to ground control.
Scan runways only after issuing takeoff or landing clearance, not before.

items.explanation: The local controller's basic duty is to visually scan runways to the maximum extent possible.

items.question_text: Check runways visually to the maximum extent possible.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That is the first direct instruction in 3-1-12.

items.question_text: Ground control must assist local control in visually scanning runways, especially when runways are in close proximity to other movement areas.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Ground control has an explicit assisting role, especially where nearby movement areas make conflicts easier to miss.

items.question_text: Ground control is responsible for assisting local control in visually scanning runways, especially when runways are close to other movement areas.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph assigns local the primary scanning duty and ground the assisting duty.

items.situation: At a complex airport, a runway sits immediately next to a busy movement area. Ground control notices a vehicle drifting toward the runway environment but assumes local will catch it because runway scanning is local's job. Local is simultaneously focused on an arrival and has not seen the movement yet.
items.para_context: Local controllers must visually scan runways to the maximum extent possible. Ground control must assist local control in visually scanning runways, especially when runways are in close proximity to other movement areas.

choices.text: Ground control should actively assist by scanning and alerting local rather than assuming runway scanning is solely local's responsibility.
Ground control should stay out of it because all runway-scanning responsibility belongs to local control alone.
Ground control should wait until the vehicle actually crosses the runway edge before assisting.
Ground control should ignore the movement if no aircraft are currently holding short.

items.explanation: The assisting role is especially important when runways are close to other movement areas and local may not have the full picture.

items.question_text: What does a controller response of aircraft call sign standby mean to an aircraft calling before entering Class D airspace?

choices.text: Two-way radio communications have been established, so the pilot may enter the Class D airspace.
Communications are not established, so the pilot must remain outside until a full traffic advisory is issued.
The pilot may enter only after receiving an explicit words enter the Delta clearance.
The pilot may enter only after the controller repeats the standby response a second time.

items.explanation: A response using the aircraft call sign followed by standby establishes two-way radio communications for Class D entry.

items.instruction: Is this Class D communications requirement correct?
items.question_text: Pilots must establish two-way radio communications before entering Class D airspace.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph begins with that pilot requirement.

items.instruction: Is this standby-response capability statement correct?
items.question_text: A controller response using the aircraft call sign and the word standby provides established two-way communications for entry into Class D airspace.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That specific response is enough to establish communications for entry.

items.instruction: Is this Class D workload condition correct?
items.question_text: If workload or traffic conditions prevent immediate provision of airport traffic control services, you must tell the pilot to remain outside the Class D airspace until conditions permit the services to be provided.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: When services cannot be provided immediately, the controller must keep the aircraft outside the Delta until they can.

items.situation: An aircraft calls the tower before entering the Delta. You are saturated and cannot provide airport traffic control services yet. Another controller says to answer with the aircraft call sign and standby, then deal with the aircraft later after it enters.
items.para_context: A standby response with the aircraft call sign establishes communications, but when workload or traffic conditions prevent immediate provision of services, the pilot must be told to remain outside the Class D airspace until conditions permit service.

choices.text: Tell the pilot to remain outside the Delta airspace and standby until conditions permit the services to be provided.
Use call sign standby alone and allow the aircraft to enter even though services cannot yet be provided.
Refuse to answer so communications are not established until you are ready.
Tell the pilot to enter and self-separate because establishing communications transfers collision-avoidance responsibility to ATC.

items.explanation: When service cannot be provided immediately, the controller must keep the aircraft outside rather than relying on standby alone.

items.situation: An aircraft calls the tower before you are ready to provide services inside the Delta. Build the instruction.
items.target_phrase: REMAIN OUTSIDE DELTA AIRSPACE AND STANDBY

items.question_text: When volcanic ash is present on the airport surface, what taxi practice should you avoid to the extent possible?

choices.text: Requiring aircraft to come to a full stop while taxiing.
Allowing aircraft to taxi continuously.
Using the longest available taxi route.
Issuing taxi instructions with route information.

items.explanation: The paragraph says to avoid requiring full stops while taxiing when ash is present on the surface.

items.instruction: Is this volcanic-ash taxi directive correct?
items.question_text: Keep aircraft taxiing without full-stop requirements when volcanic ash is present on the airport surface.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That is the first operational instruction in 3-1-14.

items.instruction: Is this volcanic-ash departure condition correct?
items.question_text: When volcanic ash is present on the airport surface, provide a rolling takeoff for departures to the extent possible.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph pairs reduced stopping on the surface with rolling takeoffs for departures.

items.instruction: Is this ash-hazard capability statement correct?
items.question_text: Following aircraft may be affected because aircraft beginning a taxi or takeoff roll on ash-contaminated surfaces will make large amounts of volcanic ash airborne again, reducing visibility and feeding ash into engines.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The note explains why stopping and restarting on ash-contaminated surfaces is hazardous to visibility and engines.

items.situation: Volcanic ash is present on the airport surface. A departure is ready to taxi, and another controller wants to build in multiple full stops before takeoff so spacing is easier to manage, even though a continuous taxi and rolling takeoff are possible.
items.para_context: When volcanic ash is present on the airport surface, avoid requiring aircraft to come to a full stop while taxiing and provide rolling takeoffs to the extent possible.

choices.text: Keep the aircraft moving as much as practical and plan for a rolling takeoff if operationally possible.
Require the planned full stops because volcanic ash changes only airborne procedures, not ground movement.
Hold the aircraft indefinitely until every visible trace of ash has been removed from the airport surface.
Use repeated stop-and-go movement because breaking the taxi into segments reduces ash ingestion risk.

items.explanation: The paragraph's ground-operations strategy is to minimize stopping and provide rolling takeoffs when possible.

items.question_text: When a pilot-in-command requests to return to the ramp, gate, or alternate deplaning area because of the Three/Four-Hour Tarmac Rule, what is the controller's basic response?

choices.text: Provide the requested service as soon as operationally practical, or advise that it cannot be accommodated because it would create a significant disruption to air traffic operations.
Deny the request automatically until the aircraft reaches the front of the departure sequence.
Grant the request only if the airport operator first suspends all other surface traffic.
Tell the pilot to remain in place until airline management contacts the tower.

items.explanation: The rule creates a practical-service default, with inability based specifically on significant operational disruption.

items.instruction: Is this tarmac-rule service directive correct?
items.question_text: Provide the requested return service as soon as operationally practical, or advise the pilot-in-command that it cannot be accommodated because it would create a significant disruption to air traffic operations.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That is the two-part controller response structure in 3-1-15.

items.question_text: When a pilot-in-command requests to return to the ramp, gate, or alternate deplaning area due to the Three/Four-Hour Tarmac Rule, you must either provide the requested service as soon as operationally practical or advise inability due to significant operational disruption.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph ties the controller response directly to that type of pilot request.

items.instruction: Is this facility-directive scope statement correct?
items.question_text: The specific actions that count as a significant disruption vary by airport and must be identified in the facility directive pertaining to the Three/Four-Hour Tarmac Rule.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The note makes significant-disruption thresholds facility-specific and directive-driven.

items.situation: A pilot-in-command requests to return to the gate because of the Three/Four-Hour Tarmac Rule. You can route the aircraft back with manageable delay and without crossing the threshold that your facility directive identifies as a significant disruption. Another controller says the request should still be denied because granting any tarmac-rule return sets a bad precedent for other crews waiting in line.
items.para_context: Provide the requested services as soon as operationally practical unless accommodating the request would create a significant disruption to air traffic operations. Significant-disruption criteria are defined in the facility directive.

choices.text: Provide the requested return service as soon as operationally practical because the request can be handled without creating the facility-defined significant disruption.
Deny it automatically because tarmac-rule requests should be refused unless the airport is otherwise empty.
Hold the aircraft in place until airline management approves the request on frequency.
Treat the request as optional and delay a decision until the pilot repeats it a second time.

items.explanation: The directive-defined disruption threshold matters; absent that threshold, the service should be provided as soon as practical.

items.situation: A tarmac-rule return request cannot be accommodated because it would create a significant operational disruption. Build the response.

items.question_text: If traffic conditions permit, what may a controller approve in Class C or Class D surface areas?
items.explanation: The paragraph allows a surface-area crossing or a speed-limit exception, but the 250-knot cap and higher-minimum-speed exception still apply.

choices.text: A request to cross the surface area or exceed the surface-area speed limit, subject to the paragraph's speed limits and exception.
Any unusual maneuver the pilot wants to perform inside the surface area.
Any speed the pilot requests, regardless of the 250-knot cap.
A buzz job if it will reduce noise over nearby neighborhoods.

items.question_text: A speed above 250 knots may be approved only if the pilot informs the controller that a higher minimum speed is required.
items.explanation: The exception to the 250-knot cap depends on the pilot reporting a higher minimum speed requirement.

items.question_text: Which type of maneuver should not be approved in Class B, C, or D surface areas unless it is essential to the performance of the flight?
items.explanation: The paragraph prohibits unusual maneuvers that are not essential to the flight.

choices.text: Unusual maneuvers such as unnecessary low passes, unscheduled flybys, or buzz jobs.
A routine straight-in approach at normal speed.
A normal transit through the surface area at authorized speed.
A standard taxi to the departure runway.

items.question_text: When does the paragraph's unusual-maneuver exception apply?
items.explanation: The exception is limited to LOA-covered activity that does not harm safety or service.

choices.text: When the activity is conducted under a letter of agreement and has no adverse effect on safety or service to other users.
Whenever a pilot requests the maneuver in advance and promises to remain in visual conditions.
Whenever the maneuver stays below 250 knots.
Whenever the airport is operating below normal arrival demand.

items.question_text: Which of the following is identified in the note as an unusual maneuver that increases hazards and noise complaints?
items.explanation: The note lists unnecessary low passes, unscheduled flybys, overly low practice approaches, and buzz jobs.

choices.text: A so-called buzz job conducted at low altitude or high speed for thrill purposes.
A normal touch-and-go conducted under tower instruction.
A routine transition through Class D at authorized speed.
A runway crossing taxi clearance by airport operations.

items.question_text: What must local controllers do regarding runway scanning?
items.explanation: Local controllers must visually scan runways to the maximum extent possible.

choices.text: Visually scan runways to the maximum extent possible.
Rely on automation displays instead of visual scanning whenever a display is available.
Scan only the departure runway and leave arrival runway scanning to ground control.
Scan runways only after issuing takeoff or landing clearance.

items.question_text: Ground control must assist local control in visually scanning runways.
items.explanation: The paragraph explicitly assigns ground control an assisting role.

items.question_text: When does the paragraph especially emphasize ground control's assisting role in runway scanning?
items.explanation: The paragraph singles out runways close to other movement areas as a special scanning concern.

choices.text: When runways are in close proximity to other movement areas.
Only at night when runway lights are on maximum intensity.
Only during IFR conditions when local control cannot issue visual separation.
Only after the local controller specifically delegates full runway responsibility to ground.

items.question_text: A runway-scanning responsibility belongs only to local control; ground control has no runway-scanning role.
items.explanation: Ground control must assist local control in visually scanning runways.

items.question_text: A vehicle is moving near a runway that sits close to another movement area, and local is focused on an arrival. What is the correct role for ground control?
items.explanation: Ground control should assist local control in visually scanning runways, especially in close-proximity layouts.

choices.text: Assist local by visually scanning and alerting local to the potential runway issue.
Assume local alone is responsible and stay out of the runway scan.
Wait until the vehicle actually crosses onto the runway before helping.
Ignore the movement if no departure has yet been cleared for takeoff.

items.question_text: What does a controller response of aircraft call sign standby mean to an aircraft calling before entering Class D airspace?
items.explanation: That response establishes two-way communications for entry into Class D airspace.

choices.text: Two-way radio communications have been established, so the pilot may enter the Class D airspace.
Communications are not yet established, so the pilot must wait outside until further contact.
The pilot may enter only after receiving an explicit enter the Delta clearance.
The pilot may enter only after squawking ident on request.

items.question_text: Pilots are required to establish two-way radio communications before entering the Class D airspace.
items.explanation: The paragraph begins with that requirement.

items.question_text: If workload or traffic conditions prevent immediate provision of airport traffic control services, what must the controller tell the pilot?
items.explanation: The pilot must be told to remain outside the Class D airspace until conditions permit the services to be provided.

choices.text: Remain outside the Class D airspace until conditions permit the services to be provided.
Enter the Class D airspace and standby for delayed service.
Proceed at pilot's discretion because standby automatically guarantees service inside the Delta.
Remain clear of all controlled airspace until approach control assumes the frequency.

items.question_text: Complete the instruction: "REMAIN OUTSIDE DELTA AIRSPACE AND _____"
items.explanation: The prescribed phraseology ends with standby.

choices.text: STANDBY

items.question_text: A controller answers an inbound Class D call with the aircraft call sign and standby, but the controller still cannot provide services immediately. What is correct?
items.explanation: Standby with the call sign establishes communications, but the pilot must still be told to remain outside if services cannot yet be provided.

choices.text: The controller must tell the pilot to remain outside the Delta until conditions permit services to be provided.
The pilot may enter automatically because standby always means services are available inside Class D.
The controller should stop transmitting so communications are no longer established.
The pilot must be transferred to approach before any remain-outside instruction can be issued.

items.question_text: When volcanic ash is present on the airport surface, what taxi practice should controllers avoid to the extent possible?
items.explanation: The paragraph says to avoid requiring aircraft to come to a full stop while taxiing.

choices.text: Requiring aircraft to come to a full stop while taxiing.
Allowing aircraft to taxi continuously.
Providing a rolling takeoff when possible.
Using the taxi route assigned by ground control.

items.question_text: When volcanic ash is present on the airport surface, provide a rolling takeoff for all departures to the extent possible.
items.explanation: The paragraph specifically directs rolling takeoffs for departures when possible.

items.question_text: Why does the note caution against full stops and repeated roll starts on ash-contaminated surfaces?
items.explanation: Taxi or takeoff roll on ash-contaminated surfaces can make ash airborne again, reducing visibility and feeding ash into following engines.

choices.text: Because newly airborne ash can reduce visibility and be ingested by the engines of following aircraft.
Because full stops automatically trigger a mandatory runway inspection under the paragraph.
Because ash prevents aircraft brakes from working below 10 knots.
Because rolling takeoffs are required only for wake turbulence spacing, not ash control.

items.question_text: This ground-operations rule applies when volcanic ash is present on the airport surface.
items.explanation: The condition for the rule is volcanic ash being present on the airport surface.

items.question_text: If continuous taxi and a rolling takeoff are operationally possible while volcanic ash is on the airport surface, what is the correct handling?
items.explanation: The paragraph directs minimizing full stops and providing rolling takeoffs when possible.

choices.text: Keep the aircraft moving as much as practical and provide the rolling takeoff.
Insert planned full stops because segmented movement lowers ash exposure.
Delay all departures until every visible trace of ash is removed from the airport surface.
Require a stop on the runway centerline before every takeoff to verify visibility once more.

items.question_text: When a pilot-in-command requests to return to the ramp, gate, or alternate deplaning area because of the Three/Four-Hour Tarmac Rule, what is the controller's basic response?
items.explanation: The controller either provides the requested service as soon as operationally practical or advises inability due to significant disruption.

choices.text: Provide the requested service as soon as operationally practical, or advise that it cannot be accommodated because it would create a significant disruption to air traffic operations.
Deny the request automatically until the aircraft reaches the head of the departure line.
Grant it only if the airport operator suspends all other surface traffic first.
Treat it as an airline issue rather than an ATC service request.

items.question_text: Facility procedures and the actions that count as a significant disruption under the Three/Four-Hour Tarmac Rule vary by airport and must be identified in the facility directive.
items.explanation: The note makes significant-disruption thresholds airport-specific and directive-based.

items.question_text: Complete the phraseology: "UNABLE DUE TO OPERATIONAL _____"
items.explanation: The prescribed unable response ends with disruption.

choices.text: DISRUPTION

items.question_text: Which phraseology option may be used when a return request cannot be accommodated because of traffic impact?
items.explanation: The paragraph provides a specific unable response for significant operational disruption.

choices.text: Unable due to operational disruption.
Unable due to tarmac rule restrictions.
Remain on taxiway and wait for airline approval.
Proceed at your discretion to any available deplaning area.

items.question_text: If your facility directive's significant-disruption threshold would not be exceeded, how should a tarmac-rule return request be handled?
items.explanation: Without the defined significant disruption, the requested service should be provided as soon as operationally practical.

choices.text: Provide the requested return service as soon as operationally practical.
Deny it anyway because all tarmac-rule return requests are presumed disruptive.
Delay the decision until airline management confirms the request with the tower.
Convert the request automatically into an expect delay advisory rather than providing the service.
